draftText: Most companies treat AI like a vending machine. You put a prompt in, and a polished paragraph comes out. The paragraph sounds smart. The paragraph never disappoints anyone. That is the entire problem with how this technology is being adopted. Boards want certainty, vendors sell certainty, and the result is a kind of institutional deodorant.

The opening paragraph does real work: it names the thing it is against and refuses to flatter the reader. The second paragraph keeps the same footing. By the third paragraph the piece is repeating itself, but the opening is strong enough to survive that.

Open-source maintainers have been sounding the alarm about unpaid labor for at least a decade. A 2018 GitHub survey found that more than 60 percent of contributors described their work as unsustainable. Recent reporting from the Linux Foundation has put a finer point on it: the average maintainer is supporting thousands of downstream users while earning nothing. The economics are not subtle.

The argument has the right shape, but the prose is doing more work than it should. The third paragraph in particular reads like a summary of the second. If we tightened that, we would give the conclusion room to land. As it stands the piece ends not because it has said what it needed to, but because the writer ran out of room.

I came in expecting a defence of a particular framework. I left understanding why the framework cannot be defended, and I had a better vocabulary for the failure. That is the most I have ever asked of a tech blog post.
The team moved very quickly through the prototype phase, and the resulting software actually just kind of works in a way that feels really quite intentional.

The argument is that an AI cannot be said to know what it does not know, because the architecture is one of pattern completion without a model of the world. A reasonable counterpoint: the human cortex is also pattern completion without a model of the world, and we say that humans know things all the time. The disagreement is not about the mechanism. It is about who is allowed to use the word.
